name: Irish
notes: Irish (Gaeilge). ~1.7M speakers (2016 census; ~80K daily L1). PERVASIVE BROAD/SLENDER DISTINCTION: every consonant has two versions (velarised 'broad' vs palatalised 'slender'), and the flanking vowel LETTERS encode which ‚ÄĒ 'caol le caol agus leathan le leathan'. Many of those flanking vowels are pure quality markers and are not pronounced (‚ü®e‚ü© before ‚ü®a o‚ü©; ‚ü®i‚ü© next to a back vowel), which is why they map to the empty string in positional_graphemes. Initial mutation: lenition (s√©imhi√ļ) and eclipsis (ur√ļ, encoded as the digraphs mb/gc/nd/bp/dt/bhf/ts). Svarabhakti epenthesis is modelled in allophone_rules. Three main dialects: Munster, Connacht, Ulster ‚ÄĒ the spec targets the Connacht/Ulster initial-stress type. An Caighde√°n Oifigi√ļil = written standard. Refs: √ď Siadhail (1989), N√≠ Chios√°in (1999), Wikipedia 'Irish orthography'/'Irish phonology'. Inventory cross-checked against N√≠ Chasaide (1999), 'Irish', Handbook of the IPA.

allophone_rules.notes: Svarabhakti (epenthesis): in Irish an unwritten short /…ô/ is inserted between a preceding ‚ü®l n r‚ü© and a following ‚ü®b bh ch f g mh‚ü© inside a single morpheme after a SHORT vowel ‚ÄĒ gorm /ňą…°…Ē…ĺň†…ômň†/, dearg /ňąd ≤a…ĺň†…ô…°/, leanbh /ňąlŐ† ≤anň†…ôw/, borb /ňąbň†…Ē…ĺň†…ôbň†/, ainm /ňąan ≤…ôm ≤/ (Wikipedia, 'Irish phonology' ¬ßEpenthesis; √ď Siadhail 1989). Modelled here as a phoneme‚Üísurface rewrite that appends the epenthetic vowel to the sonorant. LIMITATION: the engine's rule vocabulary cannot see vowel length in the preceding nucleus, so the blocking of epenthesis after a long vowel or diphthong (t√©arma /ňąt ≤eňź…ĺň†mň†…ô/) and across a morpheme boundary is NOT captured; those words are over-generated.

stress.notes: Irish (Connacht/Ulster) stresses the FIRST syllable (Wikipedia, 'Irish phonology' ¬ßProsody); unstressed short vowels reduce to /…ô/. Munster instead attracts stress to a long vowel or diphthong in the 2nd/3rd syllable and is NOT modelled here.
